title: SEGMENT 9 — April 2003: the finished sequence, what "finished" actually meant

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — April 2003: the finished sequence, what "finished" actually meant
The completion of the Human Genome Project was officially announced on April 14, 2003.
The date was deliberately chosen: April 1953 was when Watson and Crick published the double helix structure in Nature.
Fifty years to the month between the structure and the sequence.
A symbolic bookend.

The 2003 announcement was that the International Human Genome Sequencing Consortium had produced a "finished" sequence covering approximately ninety-nine percent of the gene-containing portion of the genome — the euchromatin — at an accuracy of better than one error in ten thousand bases, which is to say 99.99 percent accurate, or what is called Q40 in sequencing quality terms.
That sounds spectacular.
And it is.
But you need to understand what "finished" did and did not mean.
"Finished" meant: euchromatin only.
Euchromatin is the gene-rich, less-repetitive portion of the genome — roughly ninety-two percent of the total three billion bases.
The remaining eight percent was heterochromatin: the centromeres, the telomeres, the long arms of the acrocentric chromosomes, and the highly repetitive satellite DNA.
Those regions were not finished in 2003.

They were known to exist, they were estimated in size, but their sequence was largely a black box, because the BAC-and-shotgun approach simply could not assemble through long stretches of near-identical repeats.
"Finished" also meant: one haploid reference.
The 2003 sequence was a mosaic, not a sequence of any single person's genome.
It was assembled from BAC libraries derived from about a dozen anonymous donors, blended together.
The reference represented one possible human genome — not the genome of any actual individual, not a population, not an average.
We will return to this in Segment 11, because the inadequacy of the haploid reference for studying human variation was one of the major motivations for the follow-up HapMap and 1000 Genomes projects.
The published quality of the 2003 sequence — Q40, less than one error in ten thousand bases — was an extraordinary technical achievement.
For comparison, a single Sanger read in 1990 had a quality of maybe Q20, one error in a hundred bases.
The public consortium achieved Q40 across two-point-eight billion bases by sequencing each region multiple times, on average eight to ten times, and using the redundancy to cross-correct errors.
They also did extensive manual curation — actual humans looking at problem regions and resolving ambiguities by hand.
The "finishing" phase of the project, from the 2000 draft to the 2003 completion, was largely about closing gaps and resolving low-confidence regions through targeted re-sequencing and manual review.
The total cost of the Human Genome Project, when the books were closed, was approximately $2.7 billion in 1991 dollars — slightly under the originally projected $3 billion budget.
The total time was thirteen years from official start in October 1990 to completion announcement in April 2003 — two years under the original fifteen-year schedule.
Under budget.
Ahead of schedule.
The largest collaborative biology project ever undertaken, delivered.
The next segment is where we shift from the project itself to its scientific findings — what we actually learned from looking at the sequence.
And the headline finding, the one that surprised almost everybody, is in the gene count.
